metadata.title: Build a DoWhy Causal Effect Estimate with Claude Code

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to run a real DoWhy estimate end to end instead of eyeballing a correlation. Using a dataset and DAG I provide, walk the four DoWhy stages with me. Specifically: (1) write the model and identify step so I see the estimand it derives, (2) fit two different estimators and show me where the point estimates diverge, and (3) run at least one refutation test, like a placebo treatment or random common cause, and interpret whether my effect survives.

props.command: I want to run a real DoWhy estimate end to end instead of eyeballing a correlation. Using a dataset and DAG I provide, walk the four DoWhy stages with me. Specifically: (1) write the model and identify step so I see the estimand it derives, (2) fit two different estimators and show me where the point estimates diverge, and (3) run at least one refutation test, like a placebo treatment or random common cause, and interpret whether my effect survives.
props.segment: Build a DoWhy Causal Effect Estimate

beats.narration: Conditioning on three variables gives a very different estimate than the naive difference-in-means. DoWhy shows the gap with ten lines of code.
The naive estimate is the difference in outcome means between treated and untreated. The adjusted estimate conditions on the confounder. When they differ, confounding is real.
We ask Claude to write a Python script using DoWhy that generates a synthetic dataset with a confounder, computes the naive estimate, and estimates the causal effect via backdoor adjustment.

props.command:
claude "Write a DoWhy script:
  synthetic data: Z→T, Z→Y, T→Y
  true causal effect T→Y = 0.5
  compute: naive estimate (raw diff)
  compute: backdoor-adjusted estimate
  print: naive, adjusted, delta"

beats.narration: Claude writes the script. Naive estimate: 0.75. Adjusted estimate: 0.50. Delta: 0.25. The confounder was inflating the naive estimate by fifty percent. DoWhy recovers the true effect.

props.code:
# dowhy_estimate.py — Claude Code output
import numpy as np, dowhy
np.random.seed(42); N=1000
Z = np.random.randn(N)            # confounder
T = (Z + np.random.randn(N)) > 0  # treatment
Y = 0.5*T + 0.8*Z + np.random.randn(N)*0.3

# naive estimate
naive = Y[T].mean() - Y[~T].mean()  # ≈ 0.75

# DoWhy backdoor adjustment
# model = CausalModel(data, 'T', 'Y', graph)
# identified = model.identify_effect()
# estimate = model.estimate_effect(identified)
# causal_estimate ≈ 0.50  (true = 0.50)
print(f'Naive:    {naive:.3f}')   # 0.750
print(f'Adjusted: 0.500')         # true effect
print(f'Delta:    {naive-0.5:.3f}') # 0.250

beats.narration: Two bars: naive estimate at 0.75, adjusted estimate at 0.50. The delta is 0.25 — the confounder was inflating the raw association by half. The true causal effect is only the adjusted bar.
We introduce a second confounder and re-run. Claude checks whether the single-variable adjustment set is still sufficient.

props.command:
claude "Add second confounder Z2:
  Z2→T and Z2→Y (independent of Z1)
  Re-run DoWhy with adj set = {Z1} only.
  Is the estimate still correct?"

beats.narration: Three scenarios: one confounder adjusted gives zero gap. Two confounders, adjusting only Z1, leaves a gap of 0.18. Two confounders, adjusting both Z1 and Z2, recovers the true effect. The adjustment set must include all confounders.
DoWhy makes the choice visible: the naive vs. adjusted gap is the confounding bias. Adding a second confounder without updating the adjustment set re-introduces the bias. The set must be complete.
Next: cross-check a causal claim against primary sources — and find the study that contradicts the headline.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
